text: Hello, I'm sharing a robotic cholecystectomy. This is actually my first. This is a larger gentleman, about three hundred pounds, who had had cholangitis and pancreatitis several months earlier and was temporized with a CBD stent after ERCP clearance of the duct.

Um, I am using two right hands and one left hand, which is new to me because most of my colons and hernias, I will do the opposite way with two lefts and one right. I felt like my ports were actually in appropriate position. They're all above the umbilicus.

The surgeon adjusts the patient's position to reverse Trendelenburg on a footboard, improving visualization of the gallbladder despite the presence of visceral fat. The surgeon carefully dissects inflammatory adhesions from the bottom of the gallbladder.

The surgeon achieves improved retraction and visualization of the gallbladder, beginning dissection at the neck. The gallbladder appears edematous and thickened, presenting a visual challenge during the procedure.

I presumed that he might have occluded his cystic duct since I had last seen him, and that's why it was as thick and vascular as it was. But nonetheless, I think the case progressed reasonably nicely.

The surgeon attempts to maneuver the robotic left hand arm number one higher on the gallbladder, encountering difficulty as it bumps against the right rib cage. The arm is positioned laterally and high, which may contribute to the challenge in reaching under the ribs.
The surgeon performs further dissection around the gallbladder, gradually exposing the cystic artery, which becomes visible in the surgical field.

I also did give two mLs of ICG in the pre-upholding area about two hours prior to the surgery. I did not find that it lit the duct up very well for me. I'm not really sure why that is, but at the very least, from an anatomy standpoint, I felt comfortable with my critical view, which you'll see as I get there.
The surgeon outlines the cystic duct and the bottom of the gallbladder. A venous branch or vessel is inadvertently damaged at the back of the cystic duct, causing bleeding within the surgical field.

The surgeon attempts to create sufficient space on the cystic duct to place clips, aiming to stop the bleeding from vessels on the duct. The cystic duct and artery are visible, and various instruments, including a suction device, are used to manage unexpected bleeding.
The surgeon carefully dissects the gallbladder from the posterior surface of the liver, identifying and separating a posterior branch of the cystic artery from the gallbladder.

The surgeon uses a top-down approach to detach the remaining attachments of the gallbladder, providing a final view of the cystic artery and duct. The common bile duct is visible posteriorly in the surgical field.
The surgeon inspects the dissected areas for any signs of bleeding, focusing on the previously freed anatomical structures.
